title: Geometrical control of topology with orbital angular momentum modes
authors: Yunjia Zhai, Anselmo M. Marques, Ricardo G. Dias, Verònica Ahufinger, David Viedma
abstract: We study how the topological properties of a one-dimensional staggered lattice, loaded into states with orbital angular momentum $l=1$, can be controlled simply by tuning the relative angle between sites. The original system under consideration can be depicted as a Creutz ladder model when unwrapping the different state circulations in a synthetic dimension. Depending on the hopping strengths of the chain, different topological regimes may be accessed by changing the ladder angle, as determined by the value of the winding number of the chain. We analytically and numerically explore the different available regimes, and determine the number of topologically protected edge states that exist in each case. We also study the emergence of band inversion across topological transitions and show that it agrees with the winding number calculations, thus serving as an additional topological marker. Then, we propose a realistic experimental implementation in a photonic waveguide system, where the topological transition manifests as a sudden change of the behavior of the propagation of light in the system.
summary: This paper explores how the geometry of a 1D lattice, specifically the angle between sites, can be used to control topological phases in systems with orbital angular momentum. By mapping the system to a Creutz ladder, the authors show that tuning the angle induces transitions between different winding numbers. They propose and simulate a photonic waveguide implementation where these transitions are visible through changes in light localization.
main_problem: Investigating how the topological properties of a 1D staggered lattice can be geometrically controlled by tuning the relative angle between lattice sites using orbital angular momentum (OAM) modes.
main_result: The study demonstrates that tuning the interleg angle can induce topological phase transitions between different winding number regimes (W=0, -1, -2) and that these transitions are accompanied by observable band inversion and changes in light localization.
method: The authors use analytical derivation of the Bloch Hamiltonian and winding number, numerical exact diagonalization for energy spectra, and finite-element simulations (COMSOL) for a photonic waveguide implementation.
model_or_system: A 1D staggered lattice loaded with OAM states (l=1) that can be mapped to a Creutz ladder model in a synthetic dimension, with a proposed experimental implementation in a zig-zag array of coupled cylindrical optical waveguides.
key_observables: Winding number (W), number of topologically protected edge states, band inversion signature (sublattice-exchange operator expectation value), and light propagation/localization behavior.
important_parameters: Relative angle (phi), hopping strengths (J2, J2', J3, J3'), and the ratio of coupling strengths.
main_assumptions: The study focuses on the regime phi in (0, pi/2) and assumes the winding number is well-defined (i.e., the bulk energy gap does not close).
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the lattice and Creutz ladder mapping; Fig 2 presents phase diagrams of the winding number; Fig 3 and 6 show energy spectra and edge state emergence; Fig 7 and 8 demonstrate band structures for different lattice geometries; Fig 9 simulates electric field propagation in a waveguide with a varying angle.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical model and its mapping to a Creutz ladder, provides analytical and numerical derivations of topological invariants and phase transitions, proposes a photonic waveguide implementation, and validates the theory with light propagation simulations.
why_interesting: It demonstrates a novel way to achieve geometric control over topological phases using OAM, which is highly relevant for designing robust topological photonic circuits and synthetic dimension engineering in quantum optics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Sketch of the 1D staggered chain of sites loaded with l = 1 OAM states, with the unit cell marked with the dashed rectangle displaying two sites A and B, and with in- tracell and intercell distances d and d′, respectively. The gray line marks the origin of the phase φ0. (b) Sketch of the Creutz ladder formed by unwrapping the synthetic dimension spanned by the two circulations of the l = 1 states in each site Aj and Bj, denoted A± j and B± j . In this effective model, the unit cell has four sites. We indicate the real tunneling ampli- tudes with solid lines, and the complex tunneling amplitudes between states with different circulations with dashed lines.

FIG. 2. Winding number phase diagram of the system for the fixed coupling ratio r = J3/J2 = J′ 3/J′ 2 for (a) r = 0.7, and (b) r = 2, as a function of the angle ϕ (horizontal axis) and the coupling ratio J′ 3/J3 (vertical axis). Regions with different colors correspond to different topological phases according to the value of the winding number indicated on top.

FIG. 3. (a) Winding number W, calculated from Eq. (13), as a function of the angle ϕ. The vertical dashed line marks the critical angle ϕc = π/3 above which W is well-defined. Energy spectrum for Nc = 10 unit cells with (b) ϕ = π/5, showing a gapless case without topological edge states and (c) ϕ = 2π/5, where W = −1 and two topological edge states exist (hollow symbols). (d)–(e) Band structures for periodic boundary conditions, with the colorbar representing the signature of band inversion Sn(k), for the same angles ϕ as (b) and (c), respectively. Hopping parameter values: J2 = J′ 2 = J, J3 = J′ 3 = 2J.

FIG. 4. (a) Winding number W as a function of the angle ϕ. The vertical dashed line marks the critical angle ϕc ≈5π/16 where W changes and the red crosses mark ϕ = π/10 and ϕ = 9π/20. Energy spectrum for Nc = 10 unit cells with (b) ϕ = π/10, showing a case W = 0 without topological edge states and (c) ϕ = 9π/20, where W = −1 and two topological edge states emerge (hollow symbols). (d)–(e) Band structures for periodic boundary conditions, with the colorbar representing the signature of band inversion Sn(k), for the same angles ϕ as (b) and (c), respectively. Hopping parameter values: J2 = J, J′ 2 = 0.3J, J3 = 1.4J, J′ 3 = 0.4J.

FIG. 5. (a) Winding number W as a function of the angle ϕ. The vertical dashed line marks the critical angle ϕc ≈5π/16 where W changes and the red crosses mark ϕ = π/10 and ϕ = 9π/20. Energy spectrum for Nc = 10 unit cells with (b) ϕ = π/10, showing a case with W = −2 and four topological edge states (hollow symbols) and (c) ϕ = 9π/20, with W = −1 and two topological edge states (hollow symbols). (d)–(e) Band structures for periodic boundary conditions, with the colorbar representing the signature of band inversion Sn(k), for the same angles ϕ as (b) and (c), respectively. Hopping parameter values: J2 = J, J′ 2 = 10
